papergraph.title: Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems
papergraph.citation: Conghuan Yang et al. (2018) Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2018.2862893
papergraph.abstract: This paper proposes a co-simulation platform using field-programmable gate array (FPGA) and real-time digital simulator (RTDS) for the simulation of large power systems. It combines the advantages of high computational power from FPGA and better modelling flexibility from RTDS together. The FPGA therefore acts as an efficient and economical extension to RTDS especially when simulating large ac systems. One of the significant advantages of the proposed co-simulator is that it avoids the potential interface error existing in the conventional approach of interfacing transient stability program with electromagnetic transient programs. Two key aspects of the proposed co-simulator are discussed: 1) the interface design between FPGA and RTDS and 2) the hardware implementation and expandability of the platform. Two case studies are presented to verify the simulation accuracy and capability of the proposed co-simulator. The first case simulates a two-area four-machine power system with one area simulated in FPGA and the other area in RTDS. Comparisons are made with the case where the complete system is simulated in RTDS. The second case simulates a system of 141 buses in FPGA to demonstrate the simulator’s capability in simulating large power systems.
strengths: avoids interface error between transient stability and electromagnetic transient programs
combines high computational power of FPGA with modelling flexibility of RTDS
enables efficient and economical extension of RTDS for large AC systems
improvements: interface design between FPGA and RTDS
hardware implementation and expandability of the platform
papergraph.contribution: A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors of conventional hybrid simulation approaches and extends RTDS capability for large AC systems.

papergraph.title: Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison
papergraph.citation: Karol Najdek et al. (2026) Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3669779
papergraph.abstract: This paper proposes a data-driven feedback control framework for regulating the output voltage of a boost converter using a Nonlinear AutoRegressive model with eXogenous inputs Neural Network (NARX-NN). The NARX-NN operates as a Digital Twin Instance (DTI) that estimates the output capacitor voltage in real-time, reducing reliance on direct voltage sensing under noisy or faulty measurement conditions. Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations. The DTI is embedded within the closed-loop structure, where its output serves as a surrogate feedback signal. A systematic, information-theoretic design procedure integrates Mutual Information (MI) analysis for optimal data-sampling frequency selection, correlation and multicollinearity analysis for informative input identification, and False Nearest Neighbours (FNN) for signal-specific delay embedding. These steps ensure that the trained NARX-NN represents the converter’s nonlinear, time-varying dynamics. The case-study circuit with input and output filters exhibits nonlinear and non-minimum-phase behaviour, making it a benchmark for power-electronic control. Frequency Response Analysis (FRA) characterises dynamics across multiple load conditions. Closed-loop stability and robustness are evaluated using the $\mathfrak {D}$ -decomposition providing intuitive gain-selection criteria and stability boundaries in ( $K_{P},K_{I}$ ) space. Three feedback strategies are compared: 1) conventional measurement-based; 2) DTI-based; and 3) DTI-based and look-up-table (LUT) compensator. Simulation results confirm that the DTI-enhanced controller maintains accurate voltage regulation under dynamic conditions, achieving steady-state deviations below ±3% and robust transient performance while keeping computational effort within real-time constraints (sub-GFLOPS). The proposed framework establishes a unified foundation for integrating neural estimators into power-electronic control and advancing adaptive Digital Twin (DT) architectures for next-generation energy-conversion systems.
strengths: real-time computational efficiency (sub-GFLOPS)
robustness under sensing degradation
systematic information-theoretic design procedure
unified foundation for neural estimator integration in power-electronic control
improvements: integration of LUT compensator with DTI
multi-domain analysis including FRA and D-decomposition
papergraph.contribution: A data-driven feedback control framework using a NARX-NN-based Digital Twin Instance to estimate output capacitor voltage as a surrogate feedback signal, enabling accurate voltage regulation under noisy or faulty sensing while maintaining real-time computational feasibility.
papergraph.limitation: Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations.

gap_signals.text: Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations.

papergraph.title: Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems
papergraph.citation: Qi Xiao et al. (2025) Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems doi:10.1109/PESGM52009.2025.11225688
papergraph.venue: IEEE Power & Energy Society General Meeting
papergraph.abstract: Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids. This paper presents a scalable communication interface design and implementation to enable reliable and stable real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration. The communication interface is categorized into two types: local and remote. In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms. For remote scenarios, data exchange is achieved via internet-based file sharing or VPN-enabled communication. The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications. To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed. This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes. The implementation code is available on GitHub, providing researchers the tools to replicate and expand upon this work.

papergraph.title: Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
papergraph.citation: Kewei Shi et al. (2025) Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning doi:10.1109/IAECST68792.2025.11414900
papergraph.venue: 2025 7th International Academic Exchange Conference on Science and Technology Innovation (IAECST)
papergraph.abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy sources has led to reduced system inertia, exacerbating transient stability risks. Traditional preventive control methods, heavily reliant on time-domain simulations, struggle to meet the real-time requirements of modern power grids due to high computational burdens. To address this, this paper proposes a novel preventive control framework that integrates Deep Surrogate Models with Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization. Specifically, deep neural networks are employed to construct a digital twin environment, substituting for slow physical simulations to enable rapid state inference. Furthermore, generators are modeled as cooperative agents, utilizing a refined composite reward function to optimize control strategies. It effectively balances transient stability with thermal constraints under various fault scenarios, offering a robust and real-time decision-support tool for power system security.

Traditional preventive control methods, heavily reliant on time-domain simulations, struggle to meet the real-time requirements of modern power grids due to hig...[truncated]

papergraph.contribution: A novel preventive control framework that substitutes time-domain simulations with deep surrogate models and coordinates generator control via multi-agent reinforcement learning to achieve real-time, robust decision support.

papergraph.title: Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems
papergraph.citation: K. Yelemessov et al. (2025) Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems doi:10.3390/a18090583
papergraph.abstract: This work presents the results of a research study focused on the development and evaluation of an algorithmic optimal control framework for energy-efficient operation of screw compressors in smart power systems. The proposed approach is based on the Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP), which enables the synthesis of a mathematically grounded regulator that minimizes the total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system composed of a screw compressor and a variable-frequency induction motor. Unlike conventional PID controllers, the developed algorithm explicitly incorporates system constraints, nonlinear dynamics, and performance trade-offs into the control law, allowing for improved adaptability and energy-aware operation. Simulation results obtained using MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the PMP-based regulator outperforms classical PID solutions in both transient and steady-state regimes. Experimental tests conducted in accordance with standard energy consumption evaluation methods showed that the proposed PMP-based controller provides a reduction in specific energy consumption of up to 18% under dynamic load conditions compared to a well-tuned basic PID controller, while maintaining high control accuracy, faster settling, and complete suppression of overshoot under external disturbances. The control system demonstrates robustness to parametric uncertainty and load variability, maintaining a statistical pressure error below 0.2%. The regulator’s structure is compatible with real-time execution on industrial programmable logic controllers (PLCs), supporting integration into intelligent automation systems and smart grid infrastructures. The discrete-time PLC implementation of the regulator requires only 103 arithmetic operations per cycle and less than 102 kB of RAM for state, buffers, and logging, making it suitable for mid-range industrial controllers under 2–10 ms task cycles. Fault-tolerance is ensured via range and rate-of-change checks, residual-based plausibility tests, and safe fallbacks (baseline PID or torque-limited speed hold) in case of sensor faults. Furthermore, the proposed approach lays the groundwork for hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins. These enhancements open pathways toward predictive, self-adaptive compressor control with embedded energy optimization. The research outcomes contribute to the broader field of algorithmic control in power electronics, offering a scalable and analytically justified alternative to heuristic and empirical tuning approaches commonly used in industry. The results highlight the potential of advanced control algorithms to enhance the efficiency, stability, and intelligence of energy-intensive components within the context of Industry 4.0 and sustainable energy systems.
strengths: robustness to parametric uncertainty and load variability
real-time PLC compatibility
fault-tolerance via range/rate checks and fallbacks
reduction in specific energy consumption up to 18%
suppression of overshoot and faster settling
improvements: hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization
papergraph.scenario: screw compressors in smart power systems
papergraph.contribution: A mathematically grounded PMP-based regulator that minimizes total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system comprising a screw compressor and variable-frequency induction motor, enabling improved adaptability, energy-aware operation, and real-time PLC deployment.
papergraph.limitation: hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins

papergraph.title: Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System
papergraph.citation: Prateek Shrivastava et al. (2025) Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System doi:10.1109/ICPEE65973.2025.11411561
papergraph.venue: 2025 International Conference on Power Electronics and Energy (ICPEE)
papergraph.abstract: Single-stage grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) systems face significant challenges in maintaining voltage stability and meeting accurate power quality (PQ) standards during rapidly fluctuating irradiance, load variations, and grid disturbances. This study presents a Digital Twin (DT) architecture that enables real-time voltage regulation and PQ optimization by digitally replicating the physical PV system for predictive analysis, controller tuning, and operational decision-making. The proposed framework integrates a highly accurate physical model, analytical computations, and adaptive control algorithms driven by data to minimize total harmonic distortion (THD), maintain voltage within allowable limits, and comply with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards. The DT synchronizes real-time data from voltage and current sensors to capture dynamic system behavior, allowing for pre-deployment testing of voltage regulation strategies, including classical, adaptive, and AI-assisted methods. The architecture incorporates enhanced filtering and stability assessment techniques based on classical control and predictive & nonlinear control techniques, ensuring robustness under weak grid conditions. Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework reduces THD by more than 40%, maintains voltage within ± 1 %, and improves transient settling times compared to conventional control methods. Future work will explore the integration of DTs with distributed energy resource (DER) coordination, cyberphysical security mechanisms, and self-learning controllers to enable fully autonomous, grid-supportive PV operations in nextgeneration smart grids.

papergraph.title: IRIG-B Synchronized EMT-EMT Co-Simulation Framework for OPAL-RT/RTDS Integration
papergraph.citation: Lokesh Saravana et al. (2025) IRIG-B Synchronized EMT-EMT Co-Simulation Framework for OPAL-RT/RTDS Integration doi:10.1109/NAPS66256.2025.11272207
papergraph.abstract: This paper proposes a time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework for distributed electromagnetic transient (EMT) and EMT-domain power system simulations, integrating OPAL-RT and RTDS real-time simulators through a Transmission Line Model (TLM) interface and high-speed Aurora protocol. An IRIG-B-based synchronization scheme aligns the internal clocks of both platforms, ensuring deterministic operation and reducing numerical instabilities during hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) scenarios. The framework is evaluated on two representative test systems—a simple transmission network and an offshore wind farm connected via HVDC link—subjected to transient conditions. Comparative results demonstrate that precise time synchronization significantly improves numerical stability, minimizes solver step mismatches, and suppresses transient artifacts. The findings confirm that combining the TLM-Aurora interface with IRIG-B time synchronization provides reliable and accurate co-simulation performance for both simple and complex grid configurations, offering a scalable solution for future large-scale distributed real-time simulation and digital twin applications.
strengths: deterministic operation
reduced numerical instabilities
improved numerical stability
suppressed transient artifacts
scalable solution for large-scale distributed real-time simulation

papergraph.scenario: real-time power system simulation with OPAL-RT and RTDS hardware-in-the-loop

papergraph.contribution: A time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework enabling reliable and accurate EMT-EMT co-simulation across OPAL-RT and RTDS platforms using IRIG-B clock alignment and TLM-Aurora interface.

papergraph.title: Co-Simulation of real-time and offline power system models: An application example
papergraph.citation: Yuyao Feng et al. (2023) Co-Simulation of real-time and offline power system models: An application example doi:10.1109/ISGTEUROPE56780.2023.10408614
papergraph.venue: IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies Conference Europe
papergraph.abstract: This paper aims to provide insights into a co-simulation of phasor based and instantaneous value based simulators using a real use case. Coupling multiple high-voltage direct-current (HVDC) stations, modeled in a real-time simulation environment using RTDS simulators, to an offline RMS-Simulation in PSS®E allows for producing insights unseen before. Based on previous works, that provided the coupling mechanism, the model is tested in the smaller SAVNW system before it is implemented in the East China Transmission System consisting of over 12,000 three-phase nodes. Three HVDC connections are integrated in EMT using an individual real-time simulator each. Finally, a cross-verification is performed comparing the system response to the simulation tool BPA, where the system is modeled in a monolithic way for reference. The results of a disturbance investigation allow a combined investigation of electromagnetic transient as well as transient stability phenomena.

papergraph.title: Interpretable Human-Label-Free Deep Learning for Real-Bogus Classification with Uncertainty Quantification
papergraph.citation: Raphaël Bonnet-Guerrini et al. (2026) Interpretable Human-Label-Free Deep Learning for Real-Bogus Classification with Uncertainty Quantification arXiv:2607.05393v1
papergraph.abstract: Time-domain surveys generate many transient candidates, making Real-Bogus classification a critical step in automated discovery pipelines. Reliable labels are costly, while community labels can be noisy and survey-dependent. We aim to develop a Real-Bogus classification framework that can be trained without human-labeled data using injected transients and bogus-dominated survey data, remains robust under strong class contamination, and provides calibrated uncertainty quantification. We combine simulated transient injections with a contaminated survey class and train a dual-network model using asymmetric co-teaching for classes with different label-noise levels. We evaluate performance on a benchmark subset and analyze the learned representation with latent-space visualization tools. For uncertainty quantification (UQ), we compare MC dropout and deep ensembles and propose a low-cost hybrid strategy that exploits the dual-network setting to improve calibration. We extend the evaluation to the light-curve domain to assess recovery of light-curve classes. The method achieves strong Real-Bogus performance on the labeled subset and remains stable under severe class contamination. It recovers transient light-curve classes with high fidelity, while single-source identification is limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels. Our hybrid UQ approach achieves competitive calibration relative to more expensive ensemble baselines. Latent-space analyses indicate that uncertainty aligns with the decision boundary and reveal subclasses within the bogus population. Our results show that injection-driven, weakly supervised training can enable scalable and consistent Real-Bogus classification without human-labeled training data while providing calibrated uncertainties. The method is suited for transfer to forthcoming surveys by re-running the injection-based training pipeline.
strengths: robust under strong class contamination
provides calibrated uncertainty quantification
recovers transient light-curve classes with high fidelity
low-cost hybrid UQ strategy
reveals subclasses within bogus population
improvements: single-source identification limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels
papergraph.scenario: Real-Bogus classification in time-domain astronomical surveys
papergraph.contribution: Injection-driven, weakly supervised deep learning framework enabling human-label-free Real-Bogus classification with calibrated uncertainty quantification
papergraph.limitation: Single-source identification is limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels

papergraph.title: Digital Twin for Resilient Power Distribution Systems
papergraph.citation: Baza Somda et al. (2024) Digital Twin for Resilient Power Distribution Systems doi:10.1109/ISAP63260.2024.10744374
papergraph.venue: International Symposium on Antennas and Propagation
papergraph.abstract: Heightened awareness of the impact of climate change has led to the rapidly increasing penetration of renewable energy resources in electric energy distribution systems. Those distributed energy resources (DERs), mostly inverter-based, can act as resilience sources for the grid but also introduce new control, stability, and cybersecurity challenges. This work proposes a digital twin (DT) that combines a real-time electromagnetic transient power system simulation and a practical model for communication network simulation. The digital twin allows the testing of novel control and cybersecurity strategies, including machine learning-based anomaly detection. Simulations using the Virginia Tech Electric Service (VTES) as a test case demonstrate the capability of adequately controlled resources, including solar PV, energy storage, and a synchronous generator, to enhance resilience by providing energy to critical loads. The DERs comply with IEEE disturbance ride-through requirements, and switching transients are maintained within acceptable limits. A comprehensive DER-based resilience plan is developed and validated for the Virginia Tech Smart Grid.

papergraph.title: A Real-Time EMT Digital Model for a Dutch Regional EHV Network: Integrating Offshore Power
papergraph.citation: Jonathan Aviles-Cedeno et al. (2024) A Real-Time EMT Digital Model for a Dutch Regional EHV Network: Integrating Offshore Power doi:10.1109/IECON55916.2024.10905203
papergraph.venue: Annual Conference of the IEEE Industrial Electronics Society
papergraph.abstract: As electrical systems become increasingly complex with the integration of new electronic loads and variable renewable energy sources (VRES), modern tools are essential for their effective management and operation. This paper discusses an initial step toward the complete implementation of a digital twin for the Dutch electrical power grid: the development of a real-time digital model. This model represents the Randstad region’s electrical grid, which has recently been enhanced by substantial offshore wind power installations, including Hollandse Kust Zuid and Hollandse Kust Noord.The Real-Time Electromagnetic Transient (EMT) model described in this study enables the assessment of the impacts of offshore wind integration on network stability and power quality. Network elements have been modeled using RSCAD and implemented within the Real-Time Digital Simulator (RTDS). Detailed simulations are conducted to evaluate the grid’s capacity to handle the active and reactive power influx from the offshore wind farms. This study highlights the critical role of precise modeling in ensuring the reliability and efficiency of wind power integration into the national grid.

papergraph.title: Using Grid-Forming Energy Storage Systems to Provide Dynamic Active Power Support for Hyperscale Data Center
papergraph.citation: Brett A. Ross et al. (2026) Using Grid-Forming Energy Storage Systems to Provide Dynamic Active Power Support for Hyperscale Data Center doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3666746
papergraph.abstract: Large artificial intelligence (AI) training data centers are emerging as significant, highly dynamic digital loads that pose numerous challenges to power system stability. Methods to study and assess their impact are still being developed and have been the focus of numerous industry interest groups. Intended to contribute to bridging this gap, this paper develops a credible impact study for data centers, presenting a dynamic data-center model that includes an uninterruptible power supply (UPS), co-generator, cooling system, and a grid-forming (GFM) controlled energy storage system (ESS), with explicit converter and control dynamics. Using electromagnetic transient simulations, two stress scenarios are studied: low-voltage faults and fast load fluctuations characteristic of AI training. The results show that an adequately tuned and controlled GFM ESS can significantly improve the large load response by reducing frequency overshoot, raising the nadir, shortening settling time, and enhancing post-fault recovery. Under fast fluctuation AI training load profiles, the ESS also damps active-power oscillations and reduces torsional shaft-torque oscillations in the generator train to safe levels. These findings support ESS-based control as an effective tool for integrating large AI data centers without compromising grid stability.

papergraph.title: Real-time co-simulation of adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro for transient stability analysis
papergraph.citation: M. Mohanpurkar et al. (2018) Real-time co-simulation of adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro for transient stability analysis doi:10.1016/J.EPSR.2017.08.010
papergraph.abstract: Pumped storage hydro (PSH) based generation of electricity is a proven grid level storage technique. A new configuration i.e., adjustable speed PSH (AS-PSH) power plant is modeled and discussed in this paper. Hydrodynamic models are created using partial differential equations and the governor topology adopted from an existing, operational AS-PSH unit. Physics-based simulation of both hydrodynamics and power system dynamics has been studied individually in the past. This paper demonstrates a co-simulation of an AS-PSH unit between penstock hydrodynamics and power system events in a real-time environment. Co-simulation provides an insight into the dynamic and transient operation of AS-PSH connected to a bulk power system network. The two modes of AS-PSH operation presented in this paper are turbine and pump modes. A general philosophy of operating in turbine mode is prevalent in the field when the prices of electricity are high and in the pumping mode when prices are low. However, recently there is renewed interest in operating PSH to also provide ancillary services. A real-time co-simulation at sub-second regime of AS-PSH connected to the IEEE 14 bus test system is performed using digital real-time simulator and the results are discussed.

papergraph.title: Digital Twins for Intelligent Intersections: A Literature Review
papergraph.citation: Alben Rome Bagabaldo et al. (2025) Digital Twins for Intelligent Intersections: A Literature Review arXiv:2510.05374v1
papergraph.abstract: Intelligent intersections play a pivotal role in urban mobility, demanding innovative solutions such as digital twins to enhance safety and efficiency. This literature review investigates the integration and application of digital twins for intelligent intersections, a critical area within smart urban traffic systems. The review systematically categorizes existing research into five key thematic areas: (i) Digital Twin Architectures and Frameworks; (ii) Data Processing and Simulation Techniques; (iii) Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Adaptive Traffic Control; (iv) Safety and Protection of Vulnerable Road Users; and (v) Scaling from Localized Intersections to Citywide Traffic Networks. Each theme is explored comprehensively, highlighting significant advancements, current challenges, and critical insights. The findings reveal that multi-layered digital twin architectures incorporating real-time data fusion and AI-driven decision-making enhances traffic efficiency and safety. Advanced simulation techniques combined with sophisticated AI/ML algorithms demonstrate notable improvements in real-time responsiveness and predictive accuracy for traffic management. Additionally, the integration of digital twins has shown substantial promise in safeguarding vulnerable road users through proactive and adaptive safety strategies. Despite these advancements, key challenges persist, including interoperability of diverse data sources, scalability of digital twins for extensive traffic networks, and managing uncertainty within dynamic urban environments. Addressing these challenges will be essential for the future development and deployment of intelligent, adaptive, and sustainable intersection management systems.

papergraph.title: 从传统技艺到文化体验——曹县柳编文创产品的叙事性设计研究
papergraph.citation: 晨 张 et al. (2025) 从传统技艺到文化体验——曹县柳编文创产品的叙事性设计研究 doi:10.61369/hass.10746
papergraph.abstract: 在全面推进乡村振兴与“十四五”文化发展规划的背景下，艺术乡建逐渐成为激活乡村文化资源，推进乡村产业发展的重要路径。设计理念为乡村第三产业振兴带来新的思考。在此背景下，本文深入探讨了叙事性设计介入非遗文创的策略，契合了高质量发展要求，迎合市场对于文化价值的需求。通过提取曹县柳编文创产品的叙事基因，在多个层次丰富曹县柳编的文化体验，不仅促进了文化产业新时代的转型，也为柳编技艺的活化与非遗产业生态搭建贡献力量。

papergraph.title: 从适应到疏离：韩国留学生在华小城市的跨文化适应经历研究
papergraph.citation: Yanxin Huai (2024) 从适应到疏离：韩国留学生在华小城市的跨文化适应经历研究 doi:10.62662/kxwxz0112016
papergraph.abstract: 以一名韩国留学生为研究对象，采用质性研究中的深度访谈法，探讨了该留学生在中国东北小城市的跨文化适应经历。留学生在小城市的留学经历有三个显著特征，一是因方言引起的语言障碍；二是对教育质量的高期望未能得到满足；三是由于地理位置较为偏远引起的出行不便。基于Lysgaard的跨文化适应U型曲线理论，留学生首先会经历为期2—3个月的“蜜月阶段”，随后进入“文化冲击阶段” and “调整适应阶段”，研究发现留学生通过多种方式逐渐适应了中国的生活与文化后，会表现出对当地社会日益主动疏远的倾向。

papergraph.title: 可玩理念的乡土渐进——从城市经验到乡村实践的路径调适
papergraph.citation: 红丽 李 et al. (2026) 可玩理念的乡土渐进——从城市经验到乡村实践的路径调适 doi:10.54254/3049-7825/2026.34180
papergraph.abstract: 近年来，“可玩城市”（Playable City）作为对“智慧城市”（Smart City）的批判性回应，在西方城市研究与设计领域引起广泛关注。该理论强调市民的主动参与、游戏化机制与公共空间互动，试图将城市从“效率机器”转变为“可玩乐的舞台”。当前中国乡村振兴进程已进入下半场，一种新型的、嵌入式的改造方式——“可玩乡村”建设，有望实现乡村的可持续发展，那么，“可玩乡村”建设是否能从西方“可玩理念”中获取相关经验，通过系统梳理可玩城市的三大理论要素——智能基础设施、游戏机制与共享性相遇，分析其向中国乡村渐进所需要进行的调适。
papergraph.contribution: Title: 可玩理念的乡土渐进——从城市经验到乡村实践的路径调适 Venue: 人文社会发展 Year: 2026 Citation: 红丽 李 et al.

papergraph.title: 从文本到课堂:国际中文教材性别话语与教师调解机制研究
papergraph.citation: 思宇 谢 et al. (2026) 从文本到课堂:国际中文教材性别话语与教师调解机制研究 doi:10.65976/3080-0374.2026.05.021
papergraph.abstract: 教材作为文化传播的载体,其内置的性别表征将潜移默化地影响全球中文学习者的价值判断。研究发现,《发展汉语》教材在角色数量、职业声望、社会空间等维度存在性别失衡现象。面对该现象,教师展现出忽略、补充/修正以及批判性讨论三类主要调解机制,其选择受多因素制约。因此,教材修订应致力于构建性别平等视角;教师发展需纳入性别文化意识培训,以共同推动国际中文教育向更包容的方向发展。
papergraph.scenario: international Chinese language teaching materials | gender representation in educational texts | teacher mediation practices
papergraph.benchmark: gender imbalance across role count, occupational prestige, and social space
papergraph.contribution: identifies three teacher mediation mechanisms (ignoring, supplementing/correcting, critical discussion) in response to gendered discourse in textbooks and links their selection to multiple constraining factors
papergraph.limitation: Full text excerpt: identifies three teacher mediation mechanisms (ignoring, supplementing/correcting, critical discussion) in response to gendered discourse in textbooks and links their selection to multiple constraining factors No explicit limitation extracted.

gap_signals.text: Full text excerpt: identifies three teacher mediation mechanisms (ignoring, supplementing/correcting, critical discussion) in response to gendered discourse in textbooks and links their selection to multiple constraining factors No explicit limitation extracted.

evidence.title: Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison
evidence.citation: Karol Najdek et al. (2026) Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3669779
evidence.contribution: A data-driven feedback control framework using a NARX-NN-based Digital Twin Instance to estimate output capacitor voltage as a surrogate feedback signal, enabling accurate voltage regulation under noisy or faulty sensing while maintaining real-time computational feasibility.
evidence.limitation: Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations.

evidence.limitation: hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins

keynotes.title: Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems

keynote.title: Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems
keynote.core_problem: Interface errors and scalability limitations in conventional co-simulation approaches that couple transient stability programs with electromagnetic transient programs; insufficient computational capacity and flexibility when simulating large AC power systems in real time.
contributions: A real-time FPGA-RTDS co-simulation platform that integrates FPGA's high computational power with RTDS's modeling flexibility.
A novel interface design between FPGA and RTDS to eliminate interface errors present in conventional TSP–EMTP coupling.
A hardware-implemented, expandable co-simulation platform demonstrated on two case studies.
methods: Co-simulation architecture integrating FPGA (for computationally intensive AC system simulation) and RTDS (for flexible modeling).
Custom interface design between FPGA and RTDS to enable synchronized, error-free data exchange.
Hardware implementation of the co-simulator with emphasis on scalability and real-time performance.
experiments_or_evidence: Case study 1: Two-area four-machine system — one area simulated on FPGA, the other on RTDS; results compared against full-system RTDS simulation.
Case study 2: 141-bus system simulated entirely on FPGA to demonstrate scalability for large AC systems.
assumptions: FPGA can accurately model AC power system dynamics at required real-time resolution.
RTDS and FPGA subsystems can be synchronized reliably for co-simulation without introducing latency or phase mismatch.
The interface design preserves numerical consistency and stability across the hybrid simulation boundary.

gap_signals.text: RTDS alone lacks sufficient computational capacity for large AC systems; FPGA alone lacks modeling flexibility.
Conventional interfacing of transient stability programs with electromagnetic transient programs introduces potential interface errors.

important_claims.claim: The proposed co-simulator avoids interface errors existing in conventional TSP–EMTP coupling.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated as 'one of the significant advantages' and positioned as a motivation for the co-simulator design.

important_claims.claim: The FPGA acts as an efficient and economical extension to RTDS for large AC systems.
important_claims.evidence: Stated directly in the abstract as a core functional role of the FPGA within the architecture.

important_claims.claim: The platform is expandable and capable of simulating large-scale systems (e.g., 141 buses).
important_claims.evidence: Demonstrated via second case study where the full 141-bus system is simulated on FPGA.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a validated hardware co-simulation blueprint for real-time power system simulation that decouples computational load from modeling fidelity—enabling modular, scalable testbeds for control algorithm validation, protection scheme testing, and hardware-in-the-loop applications.

keynotes.title: Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison

keynote.title: Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison
keynote.core_problem: Regulating output voltage of a boost converter under noisy or faulty voltage sensing conditions, especially given its nonlinear, non-minimum-phase dynamics.
contributions: A data-driven feedback control framework using a NARX-NN as a Digital Twin Instance (DTI) for real-time output capacitor voltage estimation.
A systematic, information-theoretic DTI design procedure integrating Mutual Information (MI) analysis, correlation/multicollinearity analysis, and False Nearest Neighbours (FNN) for delay embedding.
Embedding the DTI within closed-loop control as a surrogate feedback signal to reduce reliance on physical voltage sensors.
Multi-domain analysis including Frequency Response Analysis (FRA) across load conditions and stability characterization via $\mathfrak{D}$-decomposition in ($K_P, K_I$) space.
Comparative evaluation of three feedback strategies: conventional measurement-based, DTI-based, and DTI-based with LUT compensator.
methods: NARX-NN modeling for real-time output capacitor voltage estimation.
Mutual Information (MI) analysis for optimal data-sampling frequency selection.
Correlation and multicollinearity analysis for informative input identification.
False Nearest Neighbours (FNN) method for signal-specific delay embedding.
$\mathfrak{D}$-decomposition for closed-loop stability analysis in ($K_P, K_I$) parameter space.
Frequency Response Analysis (FRA) across multiple load conditions.
Simulation-based comparative evaluation of three feedback strategies.
experiments_or_evidence: Simulation results showing steady-state deviations below ±3% under dynamic conditions.
Robustness validation in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations.
Transient performance evaluation confirming robustness while meeting real-time computational constraints (sub-GFLOPS).
assumptions: The boost converter’s nonlinear, time-varying dynamics can be adequately captured by a NARX-NN trained on appropriately embedded, informative input data.
The DTI surrogate feedback signal is sufficiently accurate and timely to maintain closed-loop stability and performance.
The $\mathfrak{D}$-decomposition stability boundaries reliably reflect actual closed-loop behavior under sensing degradation.
limitations: Evaluation is simulation-only; no hardware validation or experimental implementation is reported.
No quantification of DTI estimation error bounds or uncertainty propagation into control performance.
Real-time computational constraints are stated qualitatively (‘sub-GFLOPS’) without latency or resource utilization metrics.

gap_signals.text: Real-time computational constraints are stated qualitatively (‘sub-GFLOPS’) without latency or resource utilization metrics.
Evaluation is simulation-only; no hardware validation or experimental implementation is reported.
No quantification of DTI estimation error bounds or uncertainty propagation into control performance.
Lack of hardware validation limits generalizability to real power-electronic systems with switching noise, parasitics, and timing jitter.
DTI is used only for voltage estimation; no extension to joint state-parameter estimation or online adaptation is demonstrated.
No reporting of DTI estimation accuracy (e.g., RMSE, MAE) or its correlation with control performance degradation.

important_claims.claim: The DTI-enhanced controller maintains accurate voltage regulation under dynamic conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Simulation results confirm steady-state deviations below ±3% and robust transient performance.

important_claims.claim: The DTI reduces reliance on direct voltage sensing under noisy or faulty measurement conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations.

important_claims.claim: The proposed framework establishes a unified foundation for integrating neural estimators into power-electronic control.
important_claims.evidence: Systematic information-theoretic design ensures the NARX-NN represents the converter’s nonlinear, time-varying dynamics; multi-domain analysis supports generalization claims.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a reproducible, domain-informed pipeline (MI → correlation → FNN → NARX-NN → D-decomposition) for designing physics-aware neural estimators in nonlinear, non-minimum-phase systems—especially valuable for power electronics and other cyber-physical domains where sensor reliability is critical.

keynotes.title: Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning

keynote.title: Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
keynote.core_problem: Traditional preventive control methods for power system transient stability are too slow for real-time operation due to reliance on computationally expensive time-domain simulations, especially under reduced inertia from renewable integration.
contributions: A novel preventive control framework integrating deep surrogate models and multi-agent reinforcement learning.
A digital twin environment built with deep neural networks to replace time-domain simulations for rapid state inference.
Modeling generators as cooperative agents using Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) with a refined composite reward function.
methods: Deep surrogate modeling (deep neural networks) for approximating power system dynamics.
Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) for decentralized generator control.
Composite reward function balancing transient stability and thermal constraints.
experiments_or_evidence: Evaluation under various fault scenarios.
Demonstration of real-time decision-support capability and robustness.
assumptions: The deep surrogate model accurately captures essential transient dynamics of the power system.
Generators can be effectively modeled as cooperative agents with shared objectives.
Thermal constraints and stability criteria are adequately represented in the reward function.

gap_signals.text: Traditional preventive control methods struggle to meet real-time requirements due to high computational burden of time-domain simulations.
Lack of scalable, real-time-capable frameworks that jointly optimize transient stability and operational constraints like thermal limits.

important_claims.claim: The proposed framework offers a robust and real-time decision-support tool for power system security.
important_claims.evidence: It effectively balances transient stability with thermal constraints under various fault scenarios.

important_claims.claim: Deep surrogate models enable rapid state inference by substituting for slow physical simulations.
important_claims.evidence: Deep neural networks are employed to construct a digital twin environment for this purpose.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a template for coupling physics-informed surrogate modeling with multi-agent RL in safety-critical, continuous-control domains; methodology transferable to other infrastructure systems requiring real-time stability assurance.

keynotes.title: Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems

keynote.title: Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems
keynote.core_problem: Energy-inefficient operation of screw compressors in smart power systems due to limitations of conventional PID controllers in handling nonlinear dynamics, system constraints, and dynamic load conditions.
contributions: A PMP-based optimal control framework for screw compressors that explicitly incorporates nonlinear dynamics, constraints, and energy-performance trade-offs.
Demonstration of up to 18% reduction in specific energy consumption under dynamic load vs. tuned PID.
Real-time PLC-compatible implementation with low computational footprint (103 ops/cycle, <102 kB RAM).
Fault-tolerant design with sensor fault detection and safe fallback modes.
Foundation for hybrid model-AI extensions (e.g., RL, surrogate modeling, digital twins).
methods: Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP) for synthesizing an optimal regulator.
Nonlinear electromechanical modeling of screw compressor + variable-frequency induction motor.
Discrete-time implementation targeting industrial PLCs.
Range and rate-of-change checks, residual-based plausibility tests, and safe fallback strategies for fault tolerance.
experiments_or_evidence: MATLAB/Simulink simulations showing superior transient and steady-state performance vs. PID.
Experimental tests per standard energy consumption evaluation methods.
Measured statistical pressure error < 0.2% under parametric uncertainty and load variability.
Validation of real-time feasibility: execution within 2–10 ms task cycles on mid-range PLCs.
assumptions: Accurate nonlinear model of the electromechanical system is available.
Sensor measurements (e.g., pressure, speed, torque) are sufficiently reliable for feedback, barring faults handled by fallback logic.
Load dynamics fall within the operational envelope considered during PMP derivation and robustness testing.
limitations: Dependence on model fidelity — performance degrades if model mismatch exceeds robustness margin.
No reported results on long-term field deployment or aging effects.
Hybrid AI extensions (RL, digital twins) are proposed as future pathways but not implemented or evaluated in this work.

gap_signals.text: Dependence on model fidelity — performance degrades if model mismatch exceeds robustness margin.

keynotes.title: Digital Twin for Resilient Power Distribution Systems

keynote.title: Digital Twin for Resilient Power Distribution Systems
keynote.core_problem: Integrating inverter-based distributed energy resources (DERs) into power distribution systems introduces control, stability, and cybersecurity challenges that threaten grid resilience—especially under climate-induced disturbances.
contributions: A digital twin framework integrating real-time electromagnetic transient power system simulation with a practical communication network simulation.
Demonstration of DER-based resilience enhancement (e.g., supporting critical loads) using coordinated solar PV, energy storage, and synchronous generator control.
Development and validation of a comprehensive DER-based resilience plan for the Virginia Tech Smart Grid.
methods: Co-simulation of electromagnetic transient power system model and communication network model.
Implementation of machine learning-based anomaly detection within the digital twin for cybersecurity strategy testing.
Validation against IEEE disturbance ride-through requirements and switching transient limits.
experiments_or_evidence: Simulations using the Virginia Tech Electric Service (VTES) as a test case.
Demonstration that adequately controlled DERs can provide energy to critical loads during disturbances.
Verification that DERs comply with IEEE disturbance ride-through requirements and that switching transients remain within acceptable limits.

gap_signals.text: No mention of real-world deployment, field validation, or human-in-the-loop operational testing.
No explicit discussion of scalability beyond the VTES test case or generalizability to heterogeneous DER fleets with varying firmware/protocols.

datasets_or_materials: Virginia Tech Electric Service (VTES) test system

important_claims.claim: Adequately controlled DERs—including solar PV, energy storage, and a synchronous generator—can enhance grid resilience by providing energy to critical loads.
important_claims.evidence: Simulations using the VTES test case demonstrate this capability.

important_claims.claim: The proposed digital twin enables testing of novel control and cybersecurity strategies, including machine learning-based anomaly detection.
important_claims.evidence: The DT combines real-time electromagnetic transient simulation and communication network simulation to support such testing.

important_claims.claim: DERs in the validated resilience plan comply with IEEE disturbance ride-through requirements and maintain switching transients within acceptable limits.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated as demonstrated in the VTES simulations.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a co-simulation architecture blueprint for integrating power system and communication network dynamics in digital twin frameworks; test case (VTES) and resilience planning methodology are potentially transferable to other campus- or community-scale microgrids.

keynotes.title: A Real-Time EMT Digital Model for a Dutch Regional EHV Network: Integrating Offshore Power

keynote.title: A Real-Time EMT Digital Model for a Dutch Regional EHV Network: Integrating Offshore Power
keynote.core_problem: Assessing the impacts of offshore wind integration on stability and power quality in a complex, evolving EHV grid (specifically the Dutch Randstad region), requiring modern real-time simulation tools.
contributions: Development of a real-time Electromagnetic Transient (EMT) digital model for the Dutch Randstad regional EHV network
Integration of substantial offshore wind farms (Hollandse Kust Zuid and Hollandse Kust Noord) into the real-time model
Initial step toward a full digital twin implementation for the Dutch national grid
methods: Real-Time Electromagnetic Transient (EMT) modeling
Modeling using RSCAD software
Implementation and execution on the Real-Time Digital Simulator (RTDS)
experiments_or_evidence: Detailed simulations evaluating grid capacity to handle active and reactive power influx from offshore wind farms
Assessment of impacts on network stability and power quality

gap_signals.text: Described as an 'initial step toward the complete implementation' of a digital twin
Focuses only on the Randstad region—not the full Dutch national grid

datasets_or_materials: Dutch Randstad region EHV network topology
Offshore wind farm data: Hollandse Kust Zuid and Hollandse Kust Noord

important_claims.claim: Precise modeling is critical for ensuring reliability and efficiency of wind power integration into the national grid
important_claims.evidence: The study highlights this role in the context of the developed real-time EMT model and its use for stability and power quality assessment

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a replicable RTDS/RSCAD-based EMT modeling framework for regional EHV networks integrating large-scale offshore wind; serves as a foundational component for digital twin development in other jurisdictions with similar grid evolution challenges.

keynotes.title: Using Grid-Forming Energy Storage Systems to Provide Dynamic Active Power Support for Hyperscale Data Center

keynote.title: Using Grid-Forming Energy Storage Systems to Provide Dynamic Active Power Support for Hyperscale Data Center
keynote.core_problem: Hyperscale AI training data centers act as highly dynamic digital loads that threaten power system stability, and existing methods to study and assess their impact are underdeveloped.
contributions: Develops a credible dynamic data-center model incorporating UPS, co-generator, cooling system, and grid-forming (GFM) controlled energy storage system (ESS) with explicit converter and control dynamics.
Demonstrates via electromagnetic transient simulations that GFM ESS improves large-load dynamic response during low-voltage faults and fast AI-training load fluctuations.
Shows GFM ESS damps active-power oscillations and reduces torsional shaft-torque oscillations in generator trains to safe levels under AI training load profiles.
methods: Electromagnetic transient simulation
Dynamic modeling of data center subsystems (UPS, co-generator, cooling, GFM-controlled ESS) with explicit converter and control dynamics
experiments_or_evidence: Two stress scenarios simulated: (1) low-voltage faults, (2) fast load fluctuations characteristic of AI training.
Quantitative improvements observed: reduced frequency overshoot, raised frequency nadir, shortened settling time, enhanced post-fault recovery.
Observed damping of active-power oscillations and reduction of torsional shaft-torque oscillations in generator train to safe levels.

gap_signals.text: Methods to study and assess the impact of AI training data centers on power system stability are still being developed.
Existing models lack integrated, dynamic representation of data center subsystems—including UPS, co-generator, cooling, and grid-forming ESS—with explicit converter and control dynamics.

important_claims.claim: An adequately tuned and controlled GFM ESS can significantly improve the large load response during low-voltage faults.
important_claims.evidence: Results show reduced frequency overshoot, raised nadir, shortened settling time, and enhanced post-fault recovery.

important_claims.claim: Under fast fluctuation AI training load profiles, GFM ESS damps active-power oscillations and reduces torsional shaft-torque oscillations in the generator train to safe levels.
important_claims.evidence: Electromagnetic transient simulations under AI-training load profiles demonstrate damping and torque reduction.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a validated, component-explicit dynamic data-center model architecture suitable for stability analysis and co-simulation with power system models; highlights GFM ESS as a viable control enabler for integrating dynamic digital loads.

keynotes.title: Real-time co-simulation of adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro for transient stability analysis

keynote.title: Real-time co-simulation of adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro for transient stability analysis
keynote.core_problem: Lack of integrated, real-time co-simulation frameworks that jointly model penstock hydrodynamics and power system dynamics for adjustable-speed pumped storage hydro (AS-PSH) units during transient stability analysis.
contributions: First demonstration of real-time co-simulation coupling penstock hydrodynamics and power system dynamics for AS-PSH.
Modeling of AS-PSH in both turbine and pump modes using physics-based hydrodynamic PDEs and an operational governor topology.
Integration of AS-PSH into the IEEE 14-bus test system for sub-second transient stability analysis in a digital real-time simulator.
methods: Physics-based hydrodynamic modeling using partial differential equations (PDEs) for penstock dynamics.
Governor topology adopted from an existing, operational AS-PSH unit.
Real-time co-simulation framework linking hydrodynamic and power system models.
Transient stability analysis under turbine and pump operating modes.
experiments_or_evidence: Real-time co-simulation at sub-second time scale.
Implementation on a digital real-time simulator.
Case study with AS-PSH connected to the IEEE 14 bus test system.
Discussion of results showing dynamic and transient behavior in both turbine and pump modes.
assumptions: Governor topology from an existing AS-PSH unit is representative and transferable.
Hydrodynamic PDE model sufficiently captures relevant penstock transients for co-simulation fidelity.
IEEE 14-bus system serves as a valid proxy for bulk power system interactions.

gap_signals.text: Physics-based simulation of both hydrodynamics and power system dynamics has been studied individually in the past.
Recently there is renewed interest in operating PSH to also provide ancillary services.

important_claims.claim: Co-simulation provides insight into the dynamic and transient operation of AS-PSH connected to a bulk power system network.
important_claims.evidence: Real-time co-simulation at sub-second regime performed using digital real-time simulator; results discussed for turbine and pump modes.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a reusable co-simulation methodology and physics-informed modeling approach for integrating complex hydraulic-electromechanical systems into real-time power system stability studies; enables future extension to ancillary service provision analysis.

keynotes.title: Digital Twins for Intelligent Intersections: A Literature Review

keynote.title: Digital Twins for Intelligent Intersections: A Literature Review
keynote.core_problem: Enabling safe, efficient, and adaptive urban traffic management at intersections through digital twin technology, amid challenges of data interoperability, scalability, and uncertainty in dynamic urban environments.
contributions: Systematic categorization of digital twin research for intelligent intersections into five thematic areas: (i) Architectures and Frameworks; (ii) Data Processing and Simulation Techniques; (iii) AI/ML for Adaptive Traffic Control; (iv) Safety for Vulnerable Road Users; (v) Scaling to Citywide Networ...[truncated]
Synthesis of advancements, challenges, and insights across these themes
Identification of persistent technical barriers: interoperability, scalability, and uncertainty management
methods: Literature review with systematic thematic categorization
limitations: No primary empirical experiments conducted — findings derived from synthesis of existing literature
Lack of standardized evaluation metrics across reviewed studies
Limited discussion of real-world deployment constraints (e.g., infrastructure cost, legacy system integration)

gap_signals.text: Lack of standardized evaluation metrics across reviewed studies
Limited discussion of real-world deployment constraints (e.g., infrastructure cost, legacy system integration)
No primary empirical experiments conducted — findings derived from synthesis of existing literature
interoperability of diverse data sources
managing uncertainty within dynamic urban environments
scalability of digital twins for extensive traffic networks

important_claims.claim: Multi-layered digital twin architectures incorporating real-time data fusion and AI-driven decision-making enhance traffic efficiency and safety.
important_claims.evidence: Findings reveal that [...] enhances traffic efficiency and safety.

important_claims.claim: Advanced simulation techniques combined with sophisticated AI/ML algorithms improve real-time responsiveness and predictive accuracy for traffic management.
important_claims.evidence: Advanced simulation techniques combined with sophisticated AI/ML algorithms demonstrate notable improvements in real-time responsiveness and predictive accuracy for traffic management.

important_claims.claim: Digital twin integration shows substantial promise in safeguarding vulnerable road users through proactive and adaptive safety strategies.
important_claims.evidence: The integration of digital twins has shown substantial promise in safeguarding vulnerable road users through proactive and adaptive safety strategies.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a thematic taxonomy and gap inventory useful for structuring future digital twin research agendas, identifying benchmarking opportunities, and guiding interdisciplinary work at the intersection of traffic engineering, AI, and urban computing.

contributions: 揭示了‘适应后疏离’这一新现象
拓展了Lysgaard跨文化适应U型曲线理论的应用边界
methods: 质性研究
深度访谈法
单一个案研究（一名韩国留学生）

观察到适应完成后出现主动疏远当地社会的倾向
assumptions: 跨文化适应可依据Lysgaard U型曲线理论进行阶段性划分

references: Conghuan Yang et al. (2018) Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2018.2862893

evidence_type_annotations.claim: FPGA-RTDS co-simulation
A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors of conventional hybrid simulation approaches and extends RTDS capability for large AC systems.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

One of the significant advantages of the proposed co-simulator is that it avoids the potential interface error existing in the conventional approach of interfacing transient stability program with electromagnetic transient programs.
Two case studies are presented to verify the simulation accuracy and capability of the proposed co-simulator.

Full text excerpt: A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors of conventional hybrid simulation approaches and extends RTDS capability for large AC systems.

references: Karol Najdek et al. (2026) Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3669779

evidence_type_annotations.claim: machine learning model

A data-driven feedback control framework using a NARX-NN-based Digital Twin Instance to estimate output capacitor voltage as a surrogate feedback signal, enabling accurate voltage regulation under noisy or faulty sensing while maintaining real-time computational feasibility.
Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations.
Full text excerpt:

This paper proposes a data-driven feedback control framework for regulating the output voltage of a boost converter using a Nonlinear AutoRegressive model with eXogenous inputs Neural Network (NARX-NN).

Simulation results confirm that the DTI-enhanced controller maintains accurate voltage regulation under dynamic conditions, achieving steady-state deviations below ±3% and robust transient performance while keeping computational effort within real-time constraints (sub-GFLOPS).

The proposed framework establishes a unified foundation for integrating neural estimators into power-electronic control and advancing adaptive Digital Twin (DT) architectures for next-generation energy-conversion systems.

references: Qi Xiao et al. (2025) Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems doi:10.1109/PESGM52009.2025.11225688

evidence_type_annotations.claim: scalable communication interface design | real-time data extrapolation method
A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.
Challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications, including those arising from data resolution mismatches.
Full text excerpt:
Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms.
The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed.
This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes.

Full text excerpt: A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.method: Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization
references: Kewei Shi et al. (2025) Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning doi:10.1109/IAECST68792.2025.11414900

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization

A novel preventive control framework that substitutes time-domain simulations with deep surrogate models and coordinates generator control via multi-agent reinforcement learning to achieve real-time, robust decision support.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
Traditional preventive control methods, heavily reliant on time-domain simulations, struggle to meet the real-time requirements of modern power grids due to high computational burdens.
To address this, this paper proposes a novel preventive control framework that integrates Deep Surrogate Models with Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization.

Full text excerpt: A novel preventive control framework that substitutes time-domain simulations with deep surrogate models and coordinates generator control via multi-agent reinforcement learning to achieve real-time, robust decision support.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: screw compressors in smart power systems
references: K. Yelemessov et al. (2025) Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems doi:10.3390/a18090583

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP)

A mathematically grounded PMP-based regulator that minimizes total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system comprising a screw compressor and variable-frequency induction motor, enabling improved adaptability, energy-aware operation, and real-time PLC deployment.
hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins
Full text excerpt:

This work presents the results of a research study focused on the development and evaluation of an algorithmic optimal control framework for energy-efficient operation of screw compressors in smart power systems.

The proposed approach is based on the Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP), which enables the synthesis of a mathematically grounded regulator that minimizes the total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system composed of a screw compressor and a variable-frequency induction motor.

Unlike conventional PID controllers, the developed algorithm explicitly incorporates system constraints, nonlinear dynamics, and performance trade-offs into the control law, allowing for improved adaptability and energy-aware operation.
Simulation results obtained using MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the PMP-based regulator outperforms classical PID solutions in both transient and steady-state regimes.

Experimental tests conducted in accordance with standard energy consumption evaluation methods showed that the proposed PMP-based controller provides a reduction in specific energy consumption of up to 18% under dynamic load conditions compared to a well-tuned basic PID controller, while maintaining...[truncated]

Furthermore, the proposed approach lays the groundwork for hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins.

The research outcomes contribute to the broader field of algorithmic control in power electronics, offering a scalable and analytically justified alternative to heuristic and empirical tuning approaches commonly used in industry.

references: Prateek Shrivastava et al. (2025) Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System doi:10.1109/ICPEE65973.2025.11411561

evidence_type_annotations.claim: optimization algorithm

A Digital Twin framework enabling real-time voltage regulation and power quality optimization in single-stage grid-connected PV systems, validated via simulation and HIL to reduce THD by >40% and maintain voltage within ±1%

Abstract: Single-stage grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) systems face significant challenges in maintaining voltage stability and meeting accurate power quality (PQ) standards during rapidly fluctuating irradiance, load variations, and grid disturbances. This study presents a Digital Twin (DT) archit...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

The proposed framework integrates a highly accurate physical model, analytical computations, and adaptive control algorithms driven by data to minimize total harmonic distortion (THD), maintain voltage within allowable limits, and comply with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards.

The DT synchronizes real-time data from voltage and current sensors to capture dynamic system behavior, allowing for pre-deployment testing of voltage regulation strategies, including classical, adaptive, and AI-assisted methods.

Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework reduces THD by more than 40%, maintains voltage within ± 1 %, and improves transient settling times compared to conventional control methods.

Future work will explore the integration of DTs with distributed energy resource (DER) coordination, cyberphysical security mechanisms, and self-learning controllers to enable fully autonomous, grid-supportive PV operations in nextgeneration smart grids.

references: Lokesh Saravana et al. (2025) IRIG-B Synchronized EMT-EMT Co-Simulation Framework for OPAL-RT/RTDS Integration doi:10.1109/NAPS66256.2025.11272207

evidence_type_annotations.claim: IRIG-B-based time synchronization with TLM-Aurora interface for EMT-EMT co-simulation
A time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework enabling reliable and accurate EMT-EMT co-simulation across OPAL-RT and RTDS platforms using IRIG-B clock alignment and TLM-Aurora interface.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

This paper proposes a time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework for distributed electromagnetic transient (EMT) and EMT-domain power system simulations, integrating OPAL-RT and RTDS real-time simulators through a Transmission Line Model (TLM) interface and high-speed Aurora protocol.
The framework is evaluated on two representative test systems—a simple transmission network and an offshore wind farm connected via HVDC link—subjected to transient conditions.
Comparative results demonstrate that precise time synchronization significantly improves numerical stability, minimizes solver step mismatches, and suppresses transient artifacts.

Full text excerpt: A time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework enabling reliable and accurate EMT-EMT co-simulation across OPAL-RT and RTDS platforms using IRIG-B clock alignment and TLM-Aurora interface.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: Real-Bogus classification in time-domain astronomical surveys
references: Raphaël Bonnet-Guerrini et al. (2026) Interpretable Human-Label-Free Deep Learning for Real-Bogus Classification with Uncertainty Quantification arXiv:2607.05393v1

evidence_type_annotations.claim: uncertainty quantification
Injection-driven, weakly supervised deep learning framework enabling human-label-free Real-Bogus classification with calibrated uncertainty quantification
Single-source identification is limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels
Full text excerpt:

We aim to develop a Real-Bogus classification framework that can be trained without human-labeled data using injected transients and bogus-dominated survey data, remains robust under strong class contamination, and provides calibrated uncertainty quantification.
For uncertainty quantification (UQ), we compare MC dropout and deep ensembles and propose a low-cost hybrid strategy that exploits the dual-network setting to improve calibration.
The method achieves strong Real-Bogus performance on the labeled subset and remains stable under severe class contamination.
It recovers transient light-curve classes with high fidelity, while single-source identification is limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels.
Our hybrid UQ approach achieves competitive calibration relative to more expensive ensemble baselines.
Latent-space analyses indicate that uncertainty aligns with the decision boundary and reveal subclasses within the bogus population.

Our results show that injection-driven, weakly supervised training can enable scalable and consistent Real-Bogus classification without human-labeled training data while providing calibrated uncertainties.

references: Conghuan Yang et al. (2018) Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2018.2862893

evidence_type_annotations.claim: FPGA-RTDS co-simulation
A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors of conventional hybrid simulation approaches and extends RTDS capability for large AC systems.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

One of the significant advantages of the proposed co-simulator is that it avoids the potential interface error existing in the conventional approach of interfacing transient stability program with electromagnetic transient programs.
Two case studies are presented to verify the simulation accuracy and capability of the proposed co-simulator.

Full text excerpt: A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors of conventional hybrid simulation approaches and extends RTDS capability for large AC systems.

references: Karol Najdek et al. (2026) Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3669779

evidence_type_annotations.claim: machine learning model

A data-driven feedback control framework using a NARX-NN-based Digital Twin Instance to estimate output capacitor voltage as a surrogate feedback signal, enabling accurate voltage regulation under noisy or faulty sensing while maintaining real-time computational feasibility.
Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations.
Full text excerpt:

This paper proposes a data-driven feedback control framework for regulating the output voltage of a boost converter using a Nonlinear AutoRegressive model with eXogenous inputs Neural Network (NARX-NN).

Simulation results confirm that the DTI-enhanced controller maintains accurate voltage regulation under dynamic conditions, achieving steady-state deviations below ±3% and robust transient performance while keeping computational effort within real-time constraints (sub-GFLOPS).

The proposed framework establishes a unified foundation for integrating neural estimators into power-electronic control and advancing adaptive Digital Twin (DT) architectures for next-generation energy-conversion systems.
