references: Qi Xiao et al. (2025) Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems doi:10.1109/PESGM52009.2025.11225688

evidence_type_annotations.claim: scalable communication interface design | real-time data extrapolation method
A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.
Challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications, including those arising from data resolution mismatches.
Full text excerpt:
Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms.
The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed.
This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes.

Full text excerpt: A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.method: Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization
references: Kewei Shi et al. (2025) Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning doi:10.1109/IAECST68792.2025.11414900

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization

A novel preventive control framework that substitutes time-domain simulations with deep surrogate models and coordinates generator control via multi-agent reinforcement learning to achieve real-time, robust decision support.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
Traditional preventive control methods, heavily reliant on time-domain simulations, struggle to meet the real-time requirements of modern power grids due to high computational burdens.
To address this, this paper proposes a novel preventive control framework that integrates Deep Surrogate Models with Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization.

Full text excerpt: A novel preventive control framework that substitutes time-domain simulations with deep surrogate models and coordinates generator control via multi-agent reinforcement learning to achieve real-time, robust decision support.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: screw compressors in smart power systems
references: K. Yelemessov et al. (2025) Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems doi:10.3390/a18090583

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP)

A mathematically grounded PMP-based regulator that minimizes total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system comprising a screw compressor and variable-frequency induction motor, enabling improved adaptability, energy-aware operation, and real-time PLC deployment.
hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins
Full text excerpt:

This work presents the results of a research study focused on the development and evaluation of an algorithmic optimal control framework for energy-efficient operation of screw compressors in smart power systems.

The proposed approach is based on the Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP), which enables the synthesis of a mathematically grounded regulator that minimizes the total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system composed of a screw compressor and a variable-frequency induction motor.

Unlike conventional PID controllers, the developed algorithm explicitly incorporates system constraints, nonlinear dynamics, and performance trade-offs into the control law, allowing for improved adaptability and energy-aware operation.
Simulation results obtained using MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the PMP-based regulator outperforms classical PID solutions in both transient and steady-state regimes.

Experimental tests conducted in accordance with standard energy consumption evaluation methods showed that the proposed PMP-based controller provides a reduction in specific energy consumption of up to 18% under dynamic load conditions compared to a well-tuned basic PID controller, while maintaining...[truncated]

Furthermore, the proposed approach lays the groundwork for hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins.

The research outcomes contribute to the broader field of algorithmic control in power electronics, offering a scalable and analytically justified alternative to heuristic and empirical tuning approaches commonly used in industry.

references: Prateek Shrivastava et al. (2025) Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System doi:10.1109/ICPEE65973.2025.11411561

evidence_type_annotations.claim: optimization algorithm

A Digital Twin framework enabling real-time voltage regulation and power quality optimization in single-stage grid-connected PV systems, validated via simulation and HIL to reduce THD by >40% and maintain voltage within ±1%

Abstract: Single-stage grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) systems face significant challenges in maintaining voltage stability and meeting accurate power quality (PQ) standards during rapidly fluctuating irradiance, load variations, and grid disturbances. This study presents a Digital Twin (DT) archit...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

The proposed framework integrates a highly accurate physical model, analytical computations, and adaptive control algorithms driven by data to minimize total harmonic distortion (THD), maintain voltage within allowable limits, and comply with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards.

The DT synchronizes real-time data from voltage and current sensors to capture dynamic system behavior, allowing for pre-deployment testing of voltage regulation strategies, including classical, adaptive, and AI-assisted methods.

Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework reduces THD by more than 40%, maintains voltage within ± 1 %, and improves transient settling times compared to conventional control methods.

Future work will explore the integration of DTs with distributed energy resource (DER) coordination, cyberphysical security mechanisms, and self-learning controllers to enable fully autonomous, grid-supportive PV operations in nextgeneration smart grids.

references: Lokesh Saravana et al. (2025) IRIG-B Synchronized EMT-EMT Co-Simulation Framework for OPAL-RT/RTDS Integration doi:10.1109/NAPS66256.2025.11272207

evidence_type_annotations.claim: IRIG-B-based time synchronization with TLM-Aurora interface for EMT-EMT co-simulation
A time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework enabling reliable and accurate EMT-EMT co-simulation across OPAL-RT and RTDS platforms using IRIG-B clock alignment and TLM-Aurora interface.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

This paper proposes a time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework for distributed electromagnetic transient (EMT) and EMT-domain power system simulations, integrating OPAL-RT and RTDS real-time simulators through a Transmission Line Model (TLM) interface and high-speed Aurora protocol.
The framework is evaluated on two representative test systems—a simple transmission network and an offshore wind farm connected via HVDC link—subjected to transient conditions.
Comparative results demonstrate that precise time synchronization significantly improves numerical stability, minimizes solver step mismatches, and suppresses transient artifacts.

Full text excerpt: A time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework enabling reliable and accurate EMT-EMT co-simulation across OPAL-RT and RTDS platforms using IRIG-B clock alignment and TLM-Aurora interface.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: Real-Bogus classification in time-domain astronomical surveys
references: Raphaël Bonnet-Guerrini et al. (2026) Interpretable Human-Label-Free Deep Learning for Real-Bogus Classification with Uncertainty Quantification arXiv:2607.05393v1

evidence_type_annotations.claim: uncertainty quantification
Injection-driven, weakly supervised deep learning framework enabling human-label-free Real-Bogus classification with calibrated uncertainty quantification
Single-source identification is limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels
Full text excerpt:

We aim to develop a Real-Bogus classification framework that can be trained without human-labeled data using injected transients and bogus-dominated survey data, remains robust under strong class contamination, and provides calibrated uncertainty quantification.
For uncertainty quantification (UQ), we compare MC dropout and deep ensembles and propose a low-cost hybrid strategy that exploits the dual-network setting to improve calibration.
The method achieves strong Real-Bogus performance on the labeled subset and remains stable under severe class contamination.
It recovers transient light-curve classes with high fidelity, while single-source identification is limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels.
Our hybrid UQ approach achieves competitive calibration relative to more expensive ensemble baselines.
Latent-space analyses indicate that uncertainty aligns with the decision boundary and reveal subclasses within the bogus population.

Our results show that injection-driven, weakly supervised training can enable scalable and consistent Real-Bogus classification without human-labeled training data while providing calibrated uncertainties.

references: Conghuan Yang et al. (2018) Real-Time FPGA-RTDS Co-Simulator for Power Systems doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2018.2862893

evidence_type_annotations.claim: FPGA-RTDS co-simulation
A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors of conventional hybrid simulation approaches and extends RTDS capability for large AC systems.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

One of the significant advantages of the proposed co-simulator is that it avoids the potential interface error existing in the conventional approach of interfacing transient stability program with electromagnetic transient programs.
Two case studies are presented to verify the simulation accuracy and capability of the proposed co-simulator.

Full text excerpt: A real-time co-simulation platform integrating FPGA and RTDS that avoids interface errors of conventional hybrid simulation approaches and extends RTDS capability for large AC systems.

references: Karol Najdek et al. (2026) Feedback Control of a Boost Converter Using a NARX-NN-Based Digital Twin Instance: Multi-Domain Analysis and Comparison doi:10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3669779

evidence_type_annotations.claim: machine learning model

A data-driven feedback control framework using a NARX-NN-based Digital Twin Instance to estimate output capacitor voltage as a surrogate feedback signal, enabling accurate voltage regulation under noisy or faulty sensing while maintaining real-time computational feasibility.
Robustness is validated in closed-loop operation under sensing degradations.
Full text excerpt:

This paper proposes a data-driven feedback control framework for regulating the output voltage of a boost converter using a Nonlinear AutoRegressive model with eXogenous inputs Neural Network (NARX-NN).

Simulation results confirm that the DTI-enhanced controller maintains accurate voltage regulation under dynamic conditions, achieving steady-state deviations below ±3% and robust transient performance while keeping computational effort within real-time constraints (sub-GFLOPS).

The proposed framework establishes a unified foundation for integrating neural estimators into power-electronic control and advancing adaptive Digital Twin (DT) architectures for next-generation energy-conversion systems.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.method: scalable communication interface design | real-time data extrapolation method
method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: real-time co-simulation of power systems with high inverter-based resource (IBR) penetration
method_scenario_benchmark_triples.benchmark: stability | reliability | scalability | accuracy of simulation outcomes
references: Qi Xiao et al. (2025) Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems doi:10.1109/PESGM52009.2025.11225688

evidence_type_annotations.claim: scalable communication interface design | real-time data extrapolation method
A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.
Challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications, including those arising from data resolution mismatches.
Full text excerpt:
Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms.
The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed.
This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes.

Full text excerpt: A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.method: Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization
references: Kewei Shi et al. (2025) Preventive Control for Power System Transient Stability via Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning doi:10.1109/IAECST68792.2025.11414900

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Deep Surrogate Models and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization

A novel preventive control framework that substitutes time-domain simulations with deep surrogate models and coordinates generator control via multi-agent reinforcement learning to achieve real-time, robust decision support.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
Traditional preventive control methods, heavily reliant on time-domain simulations, struggle to meet the real-time requirements of modern power grids due to high computational burdens.
To address this, this paper proposes a novel preventive control framework that integrates Deep Surrogate Models with Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization.

Full text excerpt: A novel preventive control framework that substitutes time-domain simulations with deep surrogate models and coordinates generator control via multi-agent reinforcement learning to achieve real-time, robust decision support.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: screw compressors in smart power systems
references: K. Yelemessov et al. (2025) Algorithmic Optimal Control of Screw Compressors for Energy-Efficient Operation in Smart Power Systems doi:10.3390/a18090583

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP)

A mathematically grounded PMP-based regulator that minimizes total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system comprising a screw compressor and variable-frequency induction motor, enabling improved adaptability, energy-aware operation, and real-time PLC deployment.
hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins
Full text excerpt:

This work presents the results of a research study focused on the development and evaluation of an algorithmic optimal control framework for energy-efficient operation of screw compressors in smart power systems.

The proposed approach is based on the Pontryagin maximum principle (PMP), which enables the synthesis of a mathematically grounded regulator that minimizes the total energy consumption of a nonlinear electromechanical system composed of a screw compressor and a variable-frequency induction motor.

Unlike conventional PID controllers, the developed algorithm explicitly incorporates system constraints, nonlinear dynamics, and performance trade-offs into the control law, allowing for improved adaptability and energy-aware operation.
Simulation results obtained using MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the PMP-based regulator outperforms classical PID solutions in both transient and steady-state regimes.

Experimental tests conducted in accordance with standard energy consumption evaluation methods showed that the proposed PMP-based controller provides a reduction in specific energy consumption of up to 18% under dynamic load conditions compared to a well-tuned basic PID controller, while maintaining...[truncated]

Furthermore, the proposed approach lays the groundwork for hybrid extensions combining model-based control with AI-driven optimization and learning mechanisms, including reinforcement learning, surrogate modeling, and digital twins.

The research outcomes contribute to the broader field of algorithmic control in power electronics, offering a scalable and analytically justified alternative to heuristic and empirical tuning approaches commonly used in industry.

references: Prateek Shrivastava et al. (2025) Digital Twin Framework for Voltage Control and Power Quality Optimization in Single-Stage Grid-Connected Solar PV System doi:10.1109/ICPEE65973.2025.11411561

evidence_type_annotations.claim: optimization algorithm

A Digital Twin framework enabling real-time voltage regulation and power quality optimization in single-stage grid-connected PV systems, validated via simulation and HIL to reduce THD by >40% and maintain voltage within ±1%

Abstract: Single-stage grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) systems face significant challenges in maintaining voltage stability and meeting accurate power quality (PQ) standards during rapidly fluctuating irradiance, load variations, and grid disturbances. This study presents a Digital Twin (DT) archit...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

The proposed framework integrates a highly accurate physical model, analytical computations, and adaptive control algorithms driven by data to minimize total harmonic distortion (THD), maintain voltage within allowable limits, and comply with IEEE 1547 and IEC 61727 standards.

The DT synchronizes real-time data from voltage and current sensors to capture dynamic system behavior, allowing for pre-deployment testing of voltage regulation strategies, including classical, adaptive, and AI-assisted methods.

Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) evaluations demonstrate that the DT-assisted control framework reduces THD by more than 40%, maintains voltage within ± 1 %, and improves transient settling times compared to conventional control methods.

Future work will explore the integration of DTs with distributed energy resource (DER) coordination, cyberphysical security mechanisms, and self-learning controllers to enable fully autonomous, grid-supportive PV operations in nextgeneration smart grids.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: IRIG-B-based time synchronization with TLM-Aurora interface for EMT-EMT co-simulation
A time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework enabling reliable and accurate EMT-EMT co-simulation across OPAL-RT and RTDS platforms using IRIG-B clock alignment and TLM-Aurora interface.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

This paper proposes a time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework for distributed electromagnetic transient (EMT) and EMT-domain power system simulations, integrating OPAL-RT and RTDS real-time simulators through a Transmission Line Model (TLM) interface and high-speed Aurora protocol.
The framework is evaluated on two representative test systems—a simple transmission network and an offshore wind farm connected via HVDC link—subjected to transient conditions.
Comparative results demonstrate that precise time synchronization significantly improves numerical stability, minimizes solver step mismatches, and suppresses transient artifacts.

Full text excerpt: A time-synchronized hybrid co-simulation framework enabling reliable and accurate EMT-EMT co-simulation across OPAL-RT and RTDS platforms using IRIG-B clock alignment and TLM-Aurora interface.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: Real-Bogus classification in time-domain astronomical surveys
references: Raphaël Bonnet-Guerrini et al. (2026) Interpretable Human-Label-Free Deep Learning for Real-Bogus Classification with Uncertainty Quantification arXiv:2607.05393v1

evidence_type_annotations.claim: uncertainty quantification
Injection-driven, weakly supervised deep learning framework enabling human-label-free Real-Bogus classification with calibrated uncertainty quantification
Single-source identification is limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels
Full text excerpt:

We aim to develop a Real-Bogus classification framework that can be trained without human-labeled data using injected transients and bogus-dominated survey data, remains robust under strong class contamination, and provides calibrated uncertainty quantification.
For uncertainty quantification (UQ), we compare MC dropout and deep ensembles and propose a low-cost hybrid strategy that exploits the dual-network setting to improve calibration.
The method achieves strong Real-Bogus performance on the labeled subset and remains stable under severe class contamination.
It recovers transient light-curve classes with high fidelity, while single-source identification is limited by ambiguity in light-curve-derived labels.
Our hybrid UQ approach achieves competitive calibration relative to more expensive ensemble baselines.
Latent-space analyses indicate that uncertainty aligns with the decision boundary and reveal subclasses within the bogus population.

Our results show that injection-driven, weakly supervised training can enable scalable and consistent Real-Bogus classification without human-labeled training data while providing calibrated uncertainties.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: scalable communication interface design | real-time data extrapolation method
A scalable communication interface design and real-time data extrapolation method that enhances stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.
Challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications, including data resolution mismatches and instability.
Full text excerpt:
Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms.
The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed.
This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes.

Full text excerpt: A scalable communication interface design and real-time data extrapolation method that enhances stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.

comparison.prompt_assign_task: implemented as persistent create_task DAG entries with assigned agent, phase, dependency, deliverable, priority, and acceptance criteria
comparison.prompt_review_output: implemented as reviewer_gate plus boxue_final dependency gates; Boxue can add revision tasks via the task system
comparison.prompt_synthesize: implemented as bianlun_synthesis, paperwriter_draft, and boxue_final synthesis tasks
comparison.prompt_adjust_plan: implemented operationally by creating additional tasks or new delegation plans after reviewing outputs
comparison.prompt_finalize: implemented as boxue_final decision task, not as specialist execution

stronger_than_old_pipeline_tasks: role-specific tasks instead of generic phase tasks
risk constraints embedded in every task
optional teammate spawning for unblocked specialist work
prompt_alignment.remaining_manual_gate: Boxue still needs to review outputs and decide whether to create revision tasks; this preserves human/lead control over shared project state.

coordination_policy.boxue_role: decompose, assign, review, synthesize, adjust, finalize
coordination_policy.specialist_role: execute only the assigned scientific subtask
coordination_policy.shared_state: state-changing PaperGraph/project updates should be gated by lead/synthesis tasks

tasks.title: Build grounded PaperGraph evidence substrate
acceptance_criteria: At least one verifiable source or an explicit retrieval-failure report

tasks.title: Rank high-value knowledge gaps
acceptance_criteria: Every reported gap has supporting references or is flagged as ungrounded

tasks.title: Generate and evolve gap-grounded hypotheses
